query: association between Kell alloimmunization and fetal thrombocytopenia

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Alloimmunization
retrieved_docs.content: The immunogenicity of these antigens, such as the Rhesus "D" blood group, determines their ability to induce antibody formation. Other important groups are Kell, Kidd, and Duffy. Alloimmunization against RBC antigens may cause acute intravascular hemolytic transfusion reactions, DHTRs, and hemolytic disease in the fetus and newborn. Alloimmunization to platelet-specific or HLA I antigens can lead to refractoriness to platelet transfusion, posttransfusion purpura, and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia due to the mother's alloimmunization against fetal platelet antigens, most often resulting from previous pregnancies but can occur in a first pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Birth asphyxia or placental insufficiency; Congenital toxoplasmosis, rubella, cytomegalovirus, and herpes simplex virus infections; Sepsis; Congenital syphilis; Varicella; Parvovirus B19; Drug-induced thrombocytopenia due to drug-dependent antibodies; Disseminated intravascular coagulation; Thrombosis; Type 2B von Willebrand disease; Hypersplenism due to hemolytic anemia, congenital hepatitis due to viral infection, and portal vein thrombosis; Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome; Fanconi anemia; Preeclampsia; Trisomies 21, 18, and 13; Turner syndrome; Neonatal leukemia or neuroblastoma; Neonatal autoimmune thrombocytopenia due to maternal antibodies that react with both maternal and fetal platelets in systemic illnesses such as systemic lupus erythematosus; Neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia due to fetal platelets containing an antigen inherited from the father that the mother lacks; Kasabach-Merritt syndrome; and Dyskeratosis congenita. [4] [5] [10] [11] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Alloimmunization
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen-matched transfusions would effectively prevent alloimmunization. To do so, the patient's ABO, Rh, Kell, Kidd, and Duffy systems should be typed at diagnosis or before the institution of transfusion therapy. Blood should always be matched, at least with the ABO, Rh, and Kell systems.[ 2 ] Further, leukoreduction helps prevent alloimmunization. [24] Patients who have experienced alloimmunization should receive crossmatch-compatible antigen-negative RBCs. Those who experience refractoriness to platelet transfusions should avoid future platelet transfusions as much as possible. When necessary, give ABO-compatible apheresis platelets less than 48 hours old. Consider using donor platelets from family members or select HLA-matched and crossmatched platelets. Donor platelets from family members must be irradiated to prevent graft-versus-host disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Erythroid lineage suppression: The presence of Kell antigens on erythroid precursors has been proven by suppression with anti-Kell antibodies on RBC production. These antibodies inhibit the colony-forming units and cause severe fetal anemia in the case of HDFN. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Rh-Induced Hemolytic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Fisher-Race nomenclature system, the primary RBC antigens involved in blood typing are C, c, D, E, and e, and their antibodies are anti-C, anti-c, anti-D, anti-E, and anti-e. Furthermore, many antigen variants have been identified, including Cw and Du (ie, weak D) antigens, though less frequently encountered. Rh alloimmunization due to the D antigen is the most common cause of HDFN. Therefore, the Rh-positive classification refers to the presence of the D antigen, and the Rh-negative classification indicates an absent D antigen. Zygosity, or similarity of alleles, is also significant in antigen expression. Approximately 40% of Rh-positive individuals are homozygous for the D antigen (DD), and the remaining 60% are heterozygous (Dd). [10] Several other non-Rh antigen variants, including Lewis, I, Kell, Duffy, and Kidd, can also be associated with HDFN, though they differ in severity. Lewis and I are IgM antibodies and do not cross the placental barrier; therefore, HDFN does not result from these antigens. However, the Kell antibody (anti-K), typically produced following a transfusion, can result in severe HDFN with severe anemia, hydrops fetalis, and fetal death, though less hyperbilirubinemia than D alloimmunization. [11] Management of HDFN secondary to other antigenic derivations is the same as those cases caused by D alloimmunization. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombocytopenia in Pregnancy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-related TMA management is by the delivery of the fetus. Thrombocytopenia and other abnormalities may take longer to recover after the delivery of the fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Clinical Significance -- Kell Blood Group System and Disease Association
retrieved_docs.content: HDFN and neonatal jaundice: Antibodies to the Kell system can cause severe HDFN and fetal death. These IgG antibodies destroy mature fetal RBCs and the erythroid precursor cells by inhibiting erythropoiesis. In the Kell blood group-related HDFN, the degree of anemia and bilirubin levels are unrelated to the severity of the disease. [50] [51] A noninvasive prenatal test involving cell-free fetal DNA can be used for fetal antigen genotyping to diagnose HDFN. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The K antigen is detected on fetal RBCs as early as the tenth gestational week and is well-developed at birth. The KEL1 antigen is strongly immunogenic after the Rh blood group system. Study results have reported that every 1 out of 10 Kell antigen-negative individuals transfused with Kell antigen-positive donor red cells can develop anti-K antibodies after transfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The Kell blood group system is a collection of antigens found on red blood cells, erythroid cells, myeloid progenitor cells, and other tissues. These antigens are highly immunogenic, following only the ABO and Rhesus D systems in significance. The Kell system’s antigens, fully developed at birth, can provoke strong immune responses in transfusions and are implicated in hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn, as well as McLeod syndrome. The Kell antigens are detected on fetal erythrocytes as early as 10 weeks gestation, highlighting the importance of early recognition and management in pregnancy and transfusion medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed transfusion reactions often result from an amnestic response, a secondary immune reaction of the immune system to a foreign RBC antigen previously encountered. This prior exposure can occur through events such as pregnancy or previous blood transfusions. The antibody may be so weak as to escape detection on initial cross-match, but the subsequent exposure to the antigen can trigger new antibody formation. Hemolysis is mostly extravascular, of slower and longer duration, and the symptoms are less pronounced compared to an acute hemolytic reaction. The implicated antigens are usually minor antigens like Rh, Kidd, Duffy, Kell, and the MNS constituents. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic disease of the fetus and the newborn (HDFN): HDFN occurs when maternal IgG forms against fetal antigens, notably the Rh or Kell antigen. The most common type of HDFN is due to ABO incompatibility, which occurs in approximately 15% to 25% of pregnancies and tends to be less severe. [23] The incidence of positive DAT testing in ABO HDFN is very low at around 1%, and of that group, only approximately 23% of newborns develop clinically significant jaundice; hence, DAT is a poor positive predictor of newborns that require treatment. [1] [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: ABO Typing Discrepancies -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Additional clinically significant blood group systems such as the Duffy, Kell, Kidd, Lewis, and MNS can also play a role in hemolytic transfusion reactions and hemolytic disease of the fetus or newborn. Please see StatPearls' companions, " Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn ," " Kell Blood Group System ," and " Duffy Blood Group System ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombocytopenia in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombotic Microangiopathy: The management of TMA in pregnancy focuses on the antecedent cause. The thrombocytopenia is a consequence of the TMA and improves with the resolution of the underlying condition. It is critical to differentiate between pregnancy-specific TMA (PEC/HELLP/AFLP) and non-pregnancy-specific TMA, as the management of the two conditions is very different. PEC/HELLP syndrome/AFLP: The goal of treatment is the delivery of the fetus, with supportive care provided for the management of the underlying condition. [38] TTP: prompt initiation of plasma exchange helps remove the inhibitor to ADAMTS-13 and supply the patients with fresh plasma to restore the function of ADAMTS-13 and resolve hemolysis. [32] Eventually, anti-inhibitor therapy is initiated to reduce the inhibitor activity. Up to 25% of patients presenting with TTP in adulthood have a congenital absence of ADAMTS-13 (Upshaw-Schulman syndrome). [16] In such patients, anti-inhibitor therapy has no role. Such patients should receive periodic infusions of plasma. [39] Trials regarding a recombinant ADAMTS-13 are underway to circumvent the need for plasma infusions. [39] Caplacizumab (CABLIVI) was approved by the FDA in 2019 for treating adult patients with acquired TTP in combination with plasma exchange and immunosuppressive therapy. A von Willebrand factor (vWF)-directed antibody fragment targets the A1-domain of vWF and inhibits the interaction between vWF and platelets, thereby reducing both vWF-mediated platelet adhesion and platelet consumption. Its safety in pregnancy has not been established and increases the risk of hemorrhage in both mother and fetus. [40] Atypical HUS: Plasma exchange is not useful in patients with atypical HUS. The FDA has approved eculizumab for patients diagnosed with atypical HUS. [41] [42] Despite being labeled category C by the FDA, several reports have shown that eculizumab can be safely administered during pregnancy. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombocytopenia in Pregnancy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Less than 1% of pregnant patients with thrombocytopenia have a platelet count of less than 100 x 10 9 /L.

retrieved_docs.title: ABO Typing Discrepancies -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion and pregnancy are the most common causes of alloimmunization in Rh-negative individuals, resulting in the formation of antibodies against Rh antigens. These antibodies can potentially cause hemolytic transfusion reactions and place Rh-positive infants born to Rh-negative mothers at high risk of hemolytic disease of the fetus or newborn. Healthcare professionals can mitigate this risk by ensuring that Rh-negative individuals are not inadvertently exposed to Rh-positive blood or platelets. If this occurs, the patient can receive prophylactic anti-D immune globulin. Similarly, pregnant patients also receive prophylactic anti-D immune globulin. Please see StatPearls' companion, " Rh Blood Group System ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: In this course, participants gain an understanding of the functional and clinical role of the Kell blood group system, including its significance in transfusion reactions and fetal complications. Clinicians and nurses learn to recognize how immunohistological reactivities of the Kell system influence clinical outcomes and become proficient in interpreting laboratory results related to these antigens. Collaboration with an interprofessional team, including hematologists, obstetricians, and laboratory technologists, is emphasized to improve patient care, enhance diagnostic accuracy, and mitigate transfusions and hemolytic conditions risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2328
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period. They are closely linked to the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” which denotes neonatal jaundice, indicating that these entities represent the same clinical phenomenon of jaundice occurring in newborns. The condition is also related and possibly synonymous with “تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh,” suggesting an association with Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease of the newborn. In the laboratory context, measurements of total hemoglobin concentration are interpreted in light of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice; when hemoglobin values fall below the reference range, this finding is considered an important interpretive clue for the presence of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing jaundice in the newborn period, especially when Rh alloimmunization is a contributing factor.

Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are key subcategories of this spectrum; they frequently result from red‑cell destruction such as Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh) and are evaluated by laboratory parameters including total hemoglobin concentration and red blood cell count (عدد خلايا الدم الحمراء). Values below reference ranges support the diagnosis of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice. The hemorrhagic component manifests as bleeding complications documented in the neonatal period: loosened umbilical cord clamp bleeding (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), general neonatal hemorrhage (نزيف حديثي الولادة), and neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how genetic or immunologic disorders (e.g., Rh

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

These jaundice entities are closely linked to the Arabic term يرقان وليدي (neonatal jaundice) and are often associated with Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي), a condition that can also produce physiologic jaundice in the fetus or newborn. Neonatal hemorrhagic disorders are grouped under the overarching category “NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN,” which encompasses both bleeding and hemolytic pathologies. Children of this category include the aforementioned jaundice subtypes and a variety of hemorrhagic presentations: loss of umbilical cord clamp with bleeding (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑delivery umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), general newborn hemorrhage (نزيف حديثي الولادة), and neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are clinical entities that commonly arise during the fetal and/or neonatal period and can continue throughout infancy, a stage that lies within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are also known by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” reflecting their cross‑linguistic recognition. Laboratory assessment frequently includes measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, and results that fall below the reference range are interpreted as supportive of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. The disorders are closely related to, and may be synonymous with, Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh), underscoring an immuno‑hematologic mechanism of red‑cell destruction. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the significance of low hemoglobin values, and the link to Rh alloimmunization is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of affected newborns and infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 201
retrieved_docs.content: related to Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh). Neonatal (qualifier) applies to numerous specific disorders that manifest during this period, including Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, and neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system. Various jaundice phenotypes are described: breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), physiologic jaundice resulting from fetal or neonatal erythroblastosis (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), prematurity‑related physiologic jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان), and the broader category of hemolytic/perinatal jaundice. Hemorrhagic manifestations encompassed by the same parent condition include umbilical cord bleeding (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑delivery umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), general neonatal bleeding (نزيف حديثي الولادة), and respiratory‑tract

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1116
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is a specific form of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn, representing one of the many subtypes within this broader category. Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn includes several related conditions: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), and a type of jaundice associated with amniotic‑fluid‑related protection in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). Together, these entities illustrate the spectrum of hematologic and hepatic disturbances that can affect newborns, emphasizing the need for precise identification of specific disorders such as Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome to guide appropriate clinical evaluation and management.

This network of relationships highlights the interconnection of hemorrhagic events with various maternal, placental, and coagulation disorders, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing hemorrhage in pregnancy within comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 284
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are medical conditions that most frequently appear during the fetal and/or neonatal period and are especially prevalent in infancy, a specific stage within the broader period of life between birth and death. These conditions are synonymous with the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice) and are closely linked to Rh immunoprophylaxis (“تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي Rh”), reflecting the role of Rh incompatibility in their pathogenesis. Laboratory evaluation of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration and the red blood cell count (“عدد خلايا الدم الحمراء”), which are used to interpret the severity of the condition. When such test results fall below the reference range, this is interpreted as an indicator of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice. Although the condition can be observed throughout the lifespan, its primary occurrence is in the early stages of life, underscoring the importance of early detection and appropriate management in newborns and infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: (نزيف حديثي الولادة), and neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how genetic or immunologic disorders (e.g., Rh alloimmunization, Ingedikte‑galsyndroom, Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome) can produce a complex neonatal presentation that combines hemolytic anemia, diverse forms of jaundice, and bleeding tendencies, underscoring the necessity of comprehensive clinical and laboratory assessment for optimal newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how various placental positions, coagulation abnormalities, timing of hemorrhage, and associated hematologic findings converge under the single clinical concept of bleeding in pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing each subtype for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management in obstetric care.
